You have to add a user "OAuth 2.0 (Basic)":

ABAP Cloud Communication Systems Users

Key fields:

Field

Example

System ID

DSP_REST

Host Name

<your-datasphere-host> (tenant URL)

Port

443

OAuth 2.0 Endpoints

From Datasphere for Token & Authorization URL (Audience URL not required)

Client ID

Datasphere OAuth Client ID

Client Secret

Datasphere Secret (you must take note of this when creating the OAuth Client)

6 Creating a Communication Arrangement in SAP ABAP Cloud

Now we bind the Communication System to a Communication Scenario that allows REST calls.

Navigation: Communication Management → Communication Arrangements

Arrangement Example: ZDSP_CS_REST

ABAP Cloud Communication Arrangements

Note the service path: The dynamic parts of the path are set from ABAP (this is to keep the space + task chain flexible and controllable from the actual caller: ABAP).

ABAP Cloud Communication Arrangements Detailed Configuration

What this provides:

  • Preconfigured service URL
  • OAuth token handling by ABAP runtime
  • Secure outbound API consumption

7 Calling the Datasphere Task Chain API from ABAP Cloud

Now comes the fun part: consuming the REST API. Datasphere exposes endpoints such as:

  • POST /api/v1/datasphere/tasks/chains/<space_id>/run/<task_chain_technical_name>
  • GET /api/v1/datasphere/tasks/logs/<space_id>/<log_id>

Which triggers a new execution or reads the status of a task chain run.

ABAP Cloud Implementation Pattern

In ABAP Cloud when working with REST API to integrate external services, you typically:

  1. Create HTTP destination automatically from the created Communication Arrangement
  2. Use CL_WEB_HTTP_CLIENT_MANAGER
  3. Perform POST/GET requests
  4. Parse response (run ID, status)

The code snippet performs the following parts in more detail:

  1. Run as a class-run program (IF_OO_ADT_CLASSRUN) -> you can execute directly from ADT and print to the console.
  2. Locate the configured Communication Arrangement (bound to our Communication System DSP_REST) using CL_COM_ARRANGEMENT_FACTORY.
  3. Create an HTTP destination from the Communication Arrangement using CL_HTTP_DESTINATION_PROVIDER=>CREATE_BY_COMM_ARRANGEMENT.
  4. Create an HTTP client from the destination (CL_WEB_HTTP_CLIENT_MANAGER).
  5. POST request to the Task Chain “run” endpoint:
    • triggers a new run
    • receives a JSON response containing a LogId
  6. WAIT a few seconds.
  7. GET the log endpoint using the returned LogId:
    • retrieves the execution status (RUNNING, later SUCCESS / FAILED, etc.)
  8. Print everything in the ABAP Console.

ABAP-Code Snippet:

CLASS zcl_dsp_rest_api DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
   PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.

    CLASS-DATA:
      out TYPE REF TO if_oo_adt_classrun_out.
    CLASS-METHODS:
      call_dsp
        RAISING
          cx_http_dest_provider_error
          cx_web_http_client_error.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_dsp_rest_api IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    zcl_dsp_rest_api=>out = out.

    TRY.
        call_dsp( ).
      CATCH cx_web_http_client_error
            cx_http_dest_provider_error INTO DATA(exception).
        out->write( exception->get_text( ) ).
    ENDTRY.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD call_dsp.

    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_log,
             logId  TYPE i,
           END OF ty_log.

    DATA: ls_log TYPE ty_log.
    DATA(communication_system) = 'DSP_REST'.
    DATA(arrangement_factory) = cl_com_arrangement_factory=>create_instance( ).

    DATA(comm_arrangement_range) = VALUE if_com_arrangement_factory=>ty_query-cs_id_range(
      ( sign = 'I' option = 'EQ' low = communication_system ) ).

    arrangement_factory->query_ca(
      EXPORTING
        is_query = VALUE #( cs_id_range = comm_arrangement_range )
      IMPORTING
        et_com_arrangement = DATA(arrangements) ).

    DATA(arrangement) = arrangements[ 1 ].

    DATA(destination) = cl_http_destination_provider=>create_by_comm_arrangement(
      comm_scenario  = 'ZDSP_CS_REST'
      service_id     = 'ZDSP_REST_SRV_REST'
      comm_system_id = arrangement->get_comm_system_id( ) ).

    DATA(http_client) = cl_web_http_client_manager=>create_by_http_destination( destination ).
    DATA(request) = http_client->get_http_request( ).
    request->set_uri_path( '/chains/BDCPOC/run/TC_DIM2_A001' ).

    DATA(response) = http_client->execute( if_web_http_client=>post ).

    CALL METHOD /ui2/cl_json=>deserialize
      EXPORTING
        json = response->get_text( )
      CHANGING
        data = ls_log.

    data(log_str) = CONV string( ls_log-logId ).
    out->write( 'Task Chain started. LogId created:' && log_str ).

    WAIT UP TO 10 SECONDS.

    CONCATENATE '/logs/BDCPOC/' log_str INTO DATA(uri).

    clear: request, response, http_client.
    http_client = cl_web_http_client_manager=>create_by_http_destination( destination ).
    request = http_client->get_http_request( ).
    request->set_uri_path( uri ).
    response = http_client->execute( if_web_http_client=>get ).
    out->write( 'Task Chain Log REST API - via GET - Task Chain Status:' && response->get_text( ) ).

  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Once triggered:

  • Datasphere creates a new Task Chain run
  • Execution can be monitored in Datasphere UI
  • API returns run ID and status

Follow-up calls can fetch:

  • Execution status
  • Logs
  • Completion result

8 Execution Results

ABAP Cloud Console Log: Results

The Task Chain in Datasphere is in Running status:

SAP Datasphere Task Chain Running Status

9 Conclusion/Outlook & Further aspects:

Overview of the major building blocks:

Capability

Result

Secure Auth

OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials

No hardcoded secrets

via Communication Arrangements/System

Remote orchestration

Creating an end-to-end orchestrated chain of tasks

Cloud-native ABAP

Using appropriate ABAP Class APIs

Datasphere automation

Using Task Chain API

Use Cases that the implementation enables:

  • Nightly batch orchestration
  • Event-driven data loads
  • Cross-system workflows (this was our ultimate focus)
  • CI/CD data pipelines
  • API-driven analytical data refresh

References

  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Documentation: https://api.sap.com/api/DatasphereTasks/overview
  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain Help Page: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/c8a54ee704e94e15926551293243fd1d/274f2736465c4c48a091c675880502a2.html?locale=en-US
  • ABAP Cloud - how to call external APIs: https://jacekw.dev/blog/2022/oauth-client-credentials-from-abap-cloud/

These are your main takeaways to further sharpen the solution:

  • Use short token lifetimes
  • Separate technical OAuth users
  • Monitor task runs via API from ABAP
  • Handle retries & failures in ABAP
  • Log run IDs for observability

If you have any aspects, comments and/or concerns, please raise them and let's discuss. I'm happy and proud that this article was written by at least 90% human and only 10% AI 🙂

entry.title: Unlock S/4HANA Cloud Secrets: Hybrid Events+APIs Strategy for Seamless BTP Data Flows
entry.content:
Strategic Financial Data Extraction from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to SAP BTP

Using Transfer Pricing as a Real-World Example

Transfer pricing represents a perfect use case to demonstrate generic financial data extraction strategies from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Multinational organisations need GL, Controlling, Material Ledger, and Asset Accounting postings to flow reliably to external engines hosted on SAP BTP—but Public Cloud’s “no direct database access” constraint demands architecturally sound patterns.

Why Transfer Pricing Perfectly Illustrates the Challenge

The business need: Calculate inter-company markups (cost-plus, resale-minus) using real-time ACDOCA postings across multiple currencies, profit centres, and cost elements.
The constraint: Pure cloud extensibility—no RFCs, no custom ABAP, no direct table extracts. 
The solution: Three proven patterns that work for any financial data integration scenario.

Pattern 1: Event Notifications (Real-Time Push Model)

How transfer pricing uses it: GL postings trigger automatic business event notifications routed through SAP Event Mesh to BTP applications.

 

Event Notification

Generic applicability: Works for any posting-driven process—revenue recognition, inter-company reconciliation, compliance monitoring. 

High-level activation:
  1. Integration configuration apps → Activate accounting-related event notifications
  2. Communication scenarios → Configure outbound event destinations
  3. Event Mesh subscription → Point to BTP service endpoint
  4. Payload contains: Company codes, ledger amounts (local/group), profit centres, material valuations

Pattern 2: Standard Query Services (Scheduled Pull Model)

How transfer pricing uses it: BTP service queries S/4HANA nightly for new/changed financial postings.

 

Query ServiceKey services (release-dependent availability):

  • Financial line item services → GL/CO/ML/AA postings
  • Profitability services → Margin analysis data
  • Material valuation services → Cost component details

Generic applicability: Perfect for scheduled reconciliation, historical backfills, or validation runs.

Pattern 3: Hybrid Extraction (Production Resilience)

Transfer pricing demands 99.9% coverage. Smart architects layer all three patterns:

Use CasePrimary PatternTransfer Pricing ExampleGeneric Use
Real-time GL postingsEvent notificationsInvoice → immediate markupAny posting trigger
Historical catch-upStandard APIsMonth-end reconciliationData migration
Day 1 implementationBulk extractionFull ACDOCA baselineInitial loads
Custom calculationsBTP processingException overridesBusiness rules

 Three-tier reference flow:

Hybrid ExtractionBTP Implementation Patterns (Transfer Pricing Example)

Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP)
service FinancialIntegration {
  entity Postings as projection on external financial services;
  entity PricingResults as projection on HANA staging;
}

 Pattern: Event handlers + scheduled queries → unified HANA staging → business rules.

ABAP RESTful Application Programming (RAP)
define root view entity FinancialDataView
  as select from standard financial services

Pattern: Analytical CDS views over external data → transactional services.

Architect’s Production Checklist

✅ Tenant validation: Test events/APIs in your specific S/4HANA Cloud environment
✅ Quota management: Monitor Event Mesh throughput + API rate limits
✅ Fallback design: API polling validates event stream completeness
✅ Quarterly readiness: SAP evolves event coverage and API fields continuously
✅ Pure cloud: Zero custom code in production S/4HANA

The Universal Pattern

Transfer pricing proves the strategy works for complex financial scenarios. The same architecture applies to:
  • Inter-company reconciliation
  • Revenue recognition automation
  • Compliance reporting engines
  • Analytics data pipelines
  • Any standard data object/model driven process

Universal PatternKey takeaway: This isn’t “just for transfer pricing”—it’s your reusable blueprint for any S/4HANA Cloud → BTP data integration.

entry.title: Getting to a Clean Core - The new maturity model of extensions into four distinct levels (A,B,C,D)
entry.content:
Clean Core Extensibility in SAP S/4HANA

From 3‑Tier Model to Refined Extensibility Levels (A–D)

Clean Core Extensibility Definition Refinement

Introduction

Considering changing business and technology landscape, future‑proofing ERP systems is no longer optional—it is essential. SAP customers are facing constant disruptions driven by new technologies, geopolitical shifts, evolving business models, and customer expectations.

This blog help us understand why Clean Core matters, what SAP changed in the extensibility model, and how customers and partners should design extensions going forward, using the refined Clean Core Extensibility Levels (A–D).

Agenda:

  • Why Clean Core matters
  • Embrace the change
  • The contradiction: Standardization vs Differentiation
  • Clean Core principles and dimensions
  • Refined extensibility levels (A, B, C, D)
  • On‑stack vs Side‑by‑Side extensibility
  • Governance, KPIs, and Clean Core journey

Why Clean Core Matters?

Organizations fail not because they are weak, but because they cannot adapt fast enough. Large enterprises often struggle to change direction quickly due to deeply embedded, hard‑coded customizations in their ERP systems.

Let us understand using Transformation Headwinds:

Summary : Businesses must continuously respond to:

  • Process complexity
  • New technologies (AI, automation, analytics)
  • Business model changes
  • Supply chain disruptions

When business processes are tightly coupled to SAP core code, adapting to change becomes slow, risky, and expensive.

Now how to Embrace the Change

  1. Be Up‑to‑Date – Upgrade Readiness          

           SAP continuously invests in innovation. To benefit from:

    • Process improvements
    • Better user experience
    • AI and automation  

                 

Customers must stay on the latest release.
This is automatic in Public Cloud, but in Private Cloud, upgrades are customer‑managed and heavily impacted by custom code quality.

Standardization is the key enabler for smooth upgrades.

    2. Be Adaptable – Run Agile     

         Businesses also need differentiation:

    • Custom processes
    • Automation
    • Optimized workflows

         

However, differentiation traditionally comes through custom development, which increases technical debt if done incorrectly.

The Core Contradiction:                     

  • Standardization → Upgrade stability
  • Differentiation → Business Value

      

✅Clean Core is SAP’s strategy to balance both.

And What Is Clean Core ?

Clean Core refers to a set of guiding principles that support:

  • Continuous transformation
  • Agile, innovative ERP systems
  • Decoupled extensions
  • High data quality and integration     

The Five Clean Core Dimensions:              

  • Business Processes – Reduce complexity
  • Extensibility – Decouple custom code
  • Data – Controlled, high‑quality data
  • Integration – Stable, API‑based integration
  • Operations – Reliable and efficient system operation

 

👉This blog focuses primarily on Extensibility.

Evolution of the Extensibility Model 

4 Levels : 

        

High Level Recomendations :    

  • Avoid binary “clean / not clean” decisions
  • Better assess legacy custom code
  • Provide practical guidance for customers and partners

        

SAP evolved the 3‑Tier Extensibility Model into a refined level‑based model to:

  

Clean Core Extensibility Levels (A–D)

✅Level A – Clean Core (Best Practice)

  • Publicly released SAP APIs
  • SAP guarantees stability
  • Includes:
    • Side‑by‑Side extensions on SAP BTP
    • On‑stack ABAP Cloud extensions

👉Highest upgrade stability

✅Level B – Clean Core (Next Best Option)

  • Classic APIs (e.g., BAPIs, stable frameworks)
  • No formal stability contract, but SAP‑recommended

👉Still considered Clean Core

⚠️Level C – Conditionally Clean

    • Usage of SAP internal objects
    • No official SAP recommendation
    • Allowed only if no A or B option exists

SAP mitigates risk using a Change Log:

    • Tracks incompatible changes per release
    • Helps assess upgrade impact via ATC

👉Acceptable, but requires monitoring

❌Level D – Not Clean Core

  • Modifications to SAP standard
  • Direct write access to SAP tables
  • Implicit/explicit enhancements

👉Must be avoided or refactored

From 3‑Tier Model to Level Concept

Earlier:

  • Tier 1 – Cloud / Side‑by‑Side
  • Tier 2 – Wrapper‑based
  • Tier 3 – Classic ABAP

Now:

  • Legacy ABAP is differentiated into A, B, C, and D
  • Enables better upgrade stability assessment

Qualifiers :

     

Type C Tracking Via Change Log Approach : 

    

    

When to use which extensibility Model :

  

On‑Stack vs Side‑by‑Side Extensibility

On‑Stack

Used when:

  • Transactional consistency is required
  • Heavy SQL/data proximity is needed
  • Key User Extensibility is sufficient

Includes:

  • Key User tools
  • Developer Extensibility (ABAP Cloud)

Side‑by‑Side (SAP BTP)

Used when:

  • Extension lifecycle should be independent
  • Mobile or external users are involved
  • CI/CD isolation is required
  • Non‑ABAP skills (Java, Node.js, CAP)

Includes:

  • SAP Build
  • CAP
  • ABAP Environment on BTP

👉Always Level A

Choosing the Right Extension Path

SAP provides:

  • Decision trees
  • SAP Application Extension Methodology

This helps:

  • Identify business value
  • Select extension style
  • Choose the right technology building block

Examples / Use case :

How To Measure Clean Core :

      

Clean Core Measurement KPIs at a glance :

Measuring Clean Core Maturity

SAP introduced a Governance & Maturity Framework with:

  • 12 extensibility practices
  • Scoring from 0 to 5
  • Integrated into RISE with SAP methodology

Clean Core KPIs Examples 

  • Clean Core Share – Distribution of A/B/C/D
  • Technical Depth Score – Upgrade risk indicator
  • Unused Code Share – Cleanup potential
  • Business Modifications – Should trend to zero

Apply Clean Core Using 2 Notions - Get Clean & Stay Clean :

Stay Clean

  • Avoid unnecessary extensions
  • Prefer Level A (BTP first)
  • Do not add technical debt

Get Clean

  • Identify unused code
  • Refactor Level D → C/B/A
  • Improve legacy step‑by‑step

Practical Improvement Examples

      

  • Replace User Exits (Level B) with released BAdIs (Level A)
  • Replace direct table access (e.g., MARA) with released APIs

👉Improves:

  • Upgrade stability
  • Cloud readiness
  • Technical depth score

Final Recommendations

  • Establish extensibility governance
  • Upskill developers (ABAP Cloud, BTP, SAP Build)
  • Define realistic, incremental goals
  • Prefer Side‑by‑Side on SAP BTP
  • Always aim for Level A first

Conclusion

Clean Core is not about removing customization, but about building extensions the right way. With the refined extensibility model, SAP provides clarity, flexibility, and measurable progress—enabling customers and partners to innovate safely while staying upgrade‑ready.

entry.title: Early Numbering With Number Range Object In RAP
entry.content:
Introduction:

In most business applications, key fields such as Purchase Order number, Sales Order number, or Document ID are automatically generated using a number range object. This ensures uniqueness, consistency, and compliance with business standards.

In the SAP RESTful ABAP Programming Model (RAP), handling number generation requires a clear understanding of the framework lifecycle. Unlike classical ABAP, where numbers are typically generated during SAVE or in user exits, RAP introduces structured phases like Early Numbering and Late Numbering.

Early Numbering is used when the document number must be generated at the time of CREATE itself, even before the data is saved to the database.

Solution Overview:

In this blog, we will explore how to implement Early Numbering using a Number Range Object in RAP, including:

  • Creating and configuring a custom Number Range Object

  • Implementing EARLYNUMBERING_CREATE in the Behavior Implementation

  • Properly updating mapped entities using %control

  • Avoiding common mistakes like type mismatch and value reset issues

Root Entity:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'interface view for purchase header'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity zr_trv_etag as select from zpur_hdr
{
key ebeln as Ebeln,
bukrs as Bukrs,
bstyp as Bstyp,
bsart as Bsart  ,
last_changed_at as lastchangedat  
}

Projection View:

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'purchase header consumption view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity zc_pur_hdr as projection on zr_trv_etag
{
@UI.facet: [{ type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE, label: 'Purchase Header' }]
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 10 }]
    key Ebeln,
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 20 }]

    Bukrs,
    @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 30 }]
    
    Bstyp,
    @ui.lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
@UI.identification: [{ position: 40 }]
    
    Bsart
}

Interface Behavior Definition & Define Early Numbering:

managed implementation in class zbp_r_trv_etag unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;
define behavior for zr_trv_etag alias i_purhdr
persistent table ZPUR_HDR
draft table zdpurhdr
lock master total etag lastchangedat
authorization master ( instance )
early numbering
//etag master <field_name>
{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  field(readonly) Ebeln;
  draft action Activate ;
  draft action Discard;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft determine action Prepare;
  mapping for ZPUR_HDR{

  Ebeln = ebeln;
  Bukrs = bukrs;
  Bsart = bsart;
  Bstyp = bstyp;
  }
}

Early Numbering Implementation:

METHOD EARLYNUMBERING_CREATE.

   TRY.

    cl_numberrange_runtime=>number_get(
      EXPORTING
        nr_range_nr       = '01'
        object            = 'ZPUR_DOC_N'
        quantity          = 1
      IMPORTING
        number            = data(lv_pur_id)
        returncode        = DATA(lv_subrc)
        returned_quantity = DATA(lv_total_quantity)
    ).
  CATCH cx_nr_object_not_found.
  CATCH cx_number_ranges.
ENDTRY.

loop at entities into data(wa_entity) .
if WA_ENTITY-Ebeln is INITIAL.
    wa_entity-Ebeln = lv_pur_id+10(10).

    append value #( %cid     = wa_entity-%cid
                    %key     = wa_entity-%key
                    %is_draft = wa_entity-%is_draft )
      to mapped-I_PURHDR.

else.

    append value #( %cid     = wa_entity-%cid
                    %key     = wa_entity-%key
                    %is_draft = wa_entity-%is_draft )
      to mapped-I_PURHDR.
      ENDIF.
ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

Number Range Object Creation:

  • tcode : SNRO

  • give number range object.

 

 

 

Service Definition:

 

Service Binding:

 

Preview:

Create:

 

 

Conclusion:

Early Numbering in RAP allows us to generate document numbers at the time of creation itself using a Number Range Object. This is useful when the key field must be available immediately on the UI or required for draft processing. By correctly implementing the EARLYNUMBERING_CREATE method and updating the mapped entity with %control, we can ensure the generated number is properly assigned and saved. Understanding this concept helps developers avoid common mistakes and build stable, standard-compliant RAP applications.

Check Out My Other Blogs:

Topic1 :  CALL BAPI IN CUSTOM ENTITY THROUGH RAP
            Link :      CALL BAPI IN CUSTOM ENTITY THROUGH RAP
                  Topic2 :  Hide/Show Field In UI Dynamically Through Virtual Elements In RAP
            Link :       Hide/Show Field In UI Dynamically Through Virtual Elements In RAP
                         Topic3 :  RAP BASED ADOBEFORM WITH CUSTOM ACTION and Annotations
            Link :      RAP BASED ADOBEFORM WITH CUSTOM ACTION and Annotations
                        Topic4 :  Date Hierarchy Using Tree ALV(FM & OOPS )
            Link :      Date Hierarchy Using Tree ALV(FM & OOPS)
                         Topic5 :  Convert XML File to Internal Table And Download Into CSV Format In ABAP
            Link :       Convert XML File to Internal Table And Download Into CSV Format In ABAP
                         Topic6 :  Dynamic Font Styling in Adobe Forms (JavaScript)
            Link :       Dynamic Font Styling in Adobe Forms (JavaScript)
                        Topic7 :   Previewing and Downloading Adobe Forms Made Easy with ABAP
            Link :       Previewing and Downloading Adobe Forms Made Easy with ABAP

entry.title: Setting the right expectations around AI for code generation and getting past the inherent limits
entry.content:
With over two decades of working on AI, starting with my master's thesis on multi-layer perceptrons, I’m excited about the recent innovations in this area, especially as related to code generation. In this blog post, I want to share my perspective on using AI in a very specific context: coding with AI agents based on transformer models, as these are the primary tools we have at our disposal for this purpose today. I’ll start with why I use AI and then share some limitations of AI models and how to get around these pitfalls. Let’s start with a quick definition…

Generative AI is Only One Form of Artificial Intelligence

There's a lot of buzz around Artificial Intelligence (AI) these days, and it feels like every conversation about technology eventually lands on this topic. However, the term "AI" is often automatically conflated with "Generative AI" (GenAI). It's important to make a quick distinction. AI is the broad field of making machines smart. Machine Learning (ML) is a subset of AI that uses data to train models, and Deep Learning (DL) is a further subset of ML using neural networks. GenAI, which creates new content, is the latest evolution that has captured everyone's imagination.

Why I Use AI

AI is Practical

Large Language Models (LLMs) have provided us with an unprecedented natural language interface. This makes them incredibly powerful for tasks like web search and information retrieval, where a human can quickly verify the results. Coding is a domain where outputs are verifiable, which makes AI agents a significant efficiency booster. They aren't here to replace us, but to augment our abilities.

It's crucial to remember that LLMs have zero semantic understanding. They are masters of syntax, predicting the next word (or token) in a sequence through an auto-regressive process. They don't "understand" code; they predict what code should look like.

AI Adds Value to My Work

From my experience, AI brings tangible benefits to my development workflow. Here’s how:

  • Understanding Codebases: When jumping into a new or complex project, I use AI to get a quick summary of how different parts of the code interact.
  • Project Planning: Through agent orchestration, AI can help outline project structures, suggest potential components, and map out dependencies.
  • Code Documentation: This is a huge one. AI is exceptionally good at generating boilerplate documentation, docstrings, and comments, which frees me up to focus on the more complex descriptions.
  • Enforcing Guidelines: We can use AI to enforce Clean Core principles and other coding guidelines, ensuring that our code remains compliant and release-ready.

My History with AI

My journey with AI didn't start with the recent hype. It goes back to my college days, long before I entered the SAP ecosystem. My master's thesis was on multi-layer perceptrons and scaled conjugate gradient descent methods. Ever since then, I've been independently researching and following the advancements in this fascinating field.

Potential Pitfalls with AI and Take-aways

Differences Between Prompts and Target Language

Natural language is inherently ambiguous, imprecise, and often not concise. Science, and by extension software development, is about making formal, unambiguous statements. The greatest leaps in scientific history, like the formalization of algebra by Leibniz and Newton, came from moving away from rhetorical natural language and towards formal paradigms. The programming languages we use today are a natural evolution of this.

So-called "vibe coding," where one tries to build complex systems using only conversational language, is great for keynotes and simple examples but falls apart for enterprise-grade systems. I've seen attempts to "formalize" prompts to make agents more reliable, but this is a self-defeating exercise. You end up re-inventing a formal programming language, the very thing you were trying to avoid.

Take-away: This is a fundamental problem that won't be solved by simply scaling models. My practical advice is to iteratively update your agent's prompts and instructions as you code. Let the agent update its own instructions when it runs into issues. And most importantly: never commit code without verifying it yourself. Human experience is still an absolute necessity.

Predicting Syntax vs. Understanding Semantics

As mentioned, all modern coding agents are based on a transformer architecture. They predict syntax, token by token, without any semantic intelligence or understanding of programming metaconcepts. They operate on a vast joint probability distribution and can generalize well within that distribution.

However, when faced with an out-of-distribution (OOD) prompt, they "hallucinate." While syntax prediction without semantic understanding is often "good enough" to boost the efficiency of an experienced software engineer, it's irresponsible to leave critical design decisions to these agents without human oversight.

Take-away: Until a new paradigm emerges that gives AI true semantic understanding, human oversight is paramount. Used responsibly, AI is an incredibly rewarding tool that greatly supports our daily work.

Hallucinations (First and Higher Order)

OOD prompts lead to what I call "first-order hallucinations," where the LLM itself can't generalize and essentially makes things up. The answers are unreliable and require verification. Methods to minimize this include training on more data (scaling) or grounding the LLM with symbolic systems like databases or knowledge graphs.

This leads to what I term "higher-order hallucinations." The grounded data source might be perfectly accurate. However, to access it, the agent must translate your natural language prompt into a formal query. If it hallucinates during this translation, it can create an invalid query (getting no data) or a faulty query that retrieves the wrong correct data. The source is clean, but the retrieval is flawed.

Take-away: In the absence of a new paradigm, it's best to use simpler query sequences instead of one complex one. Simplify the toolset available to your agents and bundle related tasks into a single tool to minimize these translation errors.

Non-Determinism in Answers

While the forward-pass of a transformer is deterministic, the token decoding phase is not. This means if you prompt an LLM five times, you might get five different answers. For coding, this is problematic. Even with the temperature parameter set to zero, non-determinism persists.

Take-away: You never know if you've received the "best" possible generated code, and neither does the AI. This makes human verification absolutely critical, always. For mass refactoring, a robust review process is essential. Interestingly, review agents can help here, but the final decision must rest with a human.

Explainability & Verifiability

LLMs are notoriously black boxes. Billions of floating-point numbers don't offer a semantic explanation for why a particular answer was generated. Research into making these models more transparent is still in its infancy. The lack of semantic understanding, paired with non-determinism, makes true explainability incredibly difficult.

Take-away: For now, the only option for users is to wait for new research and paradigms to emerge.

TL;DR

  1. Human oversight is critical until AI gains a true semantic understanding, which it currently lacks.
  2. Use generative AI to speed your coding, but…
  3. Never commit code without verifying it yourself.
  4. Use simpler query sequences instead of one complex one.
  5. Have a robust code-review process with humans making the final decision.

Next Steps

It is irresponsible for any serious software engineer to ignore the current suite of AI tools, despite their shortcomings. Demonizing them is counter-productive. For those who know what they're doing, these tools are massive efficiency boosters. With responsible use and human oversight, they can be successfully integrated into enterprise workflows and offer many opportunities to improve the human-machine interface.

Outlook

We should not believe all the hype or inflate our expectations of what AI can realistically do today. At the same time, we shouldn't refrain from engaging with these tools in a meaningful way. We, the developers, are still the ones with the creativity, innovation, and semantic understanding. Let's use these tools to amplify that.

Happy coding!

What are your thoughts? Share your experiences with AI in the comments below!

entry.title: From Business Objects to Business Flows: The Missing Layer of RAP Documentation
entry.content:
Part 2 in a series on modern SAP documentation.

Part 1 covered Business Object Canvases, ADRs, and dependency matrices. This post tackles what happens when you need to understand how it all fits together.

In my last post, I argued that RICEFW documentation doesn't fit how we build with RAP. Business Object Canvases, ADRs, and dependency matrices give us a better way to document individual capabilities and the decisions behind them.

But I ended with an open question: what happens when someone asks “Walk me through what happens when a customer creates an order”? You point them to four BO Canvases. They read all four. They still don't understand the sequence — what triggers what, in which order, and what happens when something fails.

Functional Specs Describe Pieces, Not Flows

Our team documents in Confluence today. Functional specifications per RICEFW object — one for the report, another for the interface, another for the enhancement, another for the form. They're organized around technical artifacts, not business capabilities.

So if you want to understand the Sales Order, you're reading across half a dozen specs. The enhancement spec for validation, the interface spec for pricing, the form spec for order confirmation. Each makes sense on its own. But piecing together what actually happens when a customer creates an order means jumping between documents that were never designed to tell that story.

Questions like these are hard to answer:

What happens after the sales order is saved? What events fire, and in what order? If credit check fails, does the order stay blocked, or does it roll back? Which steps are synchronous and which are async? Where does pricing fit — before, during, or after save?

The answers live in people's heads, scattered across specs, or buried in code.

Why RAP Makes This Worse — and Better

In classic ABAP, process orchestration was often implicit. User exits, BAdIs, and function modules wired through transaction codes. You could trace the flow by reading the code.

RAP changes this. Business objects are decoupled. They communicate through events, EML calls, and service projections. Good architecture — but the process flow is no longer visible in any single place. The good news: because RAP forces you to think in events and clear BO boundaries, the sequence can be documented cleanly. You just need to actually do it.

An Idea: Sequence Maps

What I think we need is something simple — a visual flow that shows the chain of BOs in a business process, the trigger between each step, and whether it's synchronous or asynchronous. Nothing fancy. A diagram in Confluence: Customer creates order → Sales Order BO (SAVE) → [sync] Pricing determination → [sync] Credit check → [event] OrderCreated → [async] Inventory reservation → [async] Shipping notification

Even something this basic should be valuable. New developers could see the flow in thirty seconds. Architects could spot the sync/async boundaries. Application experts could validate whether the sequence matches what they expect.

The Full Picture

Each documentation layer answers a different question:

  • BO Canvas: What does this business object do?
  • ADR: Why did we build it this way?
  • Dependency matrix: What's connected to what?
  • Sequence map: What happens when a business process runs?

The sequence mapping is the part I haven't started yet — but it's the gap I feel most in daily work. Too many “go ask Henrik, he knows” conversations. If you're on a similar journey — moving from RICEFW specs to something that fits RAP — I'd like to hear what you've tried.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Learning Map
entry.content:
The ABAP Cloud development model (ABAP Cloud) is the foundation of ABAP-based development at SAP, its partners, and customers, designed from the ground up to adhere to clean core principles. It brings together evolving technologies and methodologies into a sustainable, long-lasting programming model. 

The ABAP Cloud development model encompasses all aspects of modern cloud-ready software development, resulting in a wide range of topics that need to be understood. Therefore, clear guidance is helpful for getting started with and navigating ABAP Cloud.

Discover the ABAP Cloud Learning Map🗺️ – your guide to mastering the ABAP Cloud development model. It provides an overview of essential topics and skills, along with a curated collection of links to key resources covering the technologies, tools, approaches, and practices that drive cloud-ready, upgrade-stable ABAP development. 📚Whether you’re starting out or filling knowledge gaps, the learning map helps you navigate modern ABAP Cloud development at your own pace. 

🔗Access it here: ABAP Cloud Learning Map↗

entry.title: IoT: Air Quality Dashboard with SAP RAP and Mobile App
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

After my last blog about IoT and SAP CAP, I’d like to share a new pet project combining IoT and SAP ABAP RAP.
Last summer was extremely hot, and I kept wondering when it would be best to open the windows in our meeting room to get some fresh air. That led me to another question: could I measure the air quality and integrate the data with SAP Cloud Platform?
Elevated concentrations of carbon dioxide (CO2) and insufficient ventilation in indoor environments can decrease cognitive performance and may cause headaches. CO2 detectors are used to monitor concentrations and provide a warning when predefined thresholds are exceeded. Carbon dioxide concentration is expressed in ppm (parts per million) which represents the number of carbon dioxide molecules per one million molecules of air.

Indoor air quality is classified according to the following concentration ranges:

  • at concentration below 800ppm, air quality is considered good;
  • between 800 and 1000ppm, slight stuffiness may be perceived;
  • between 1000 and 1500ppm, air quality is poor and cognitive performance may decrease;
  • between 1500 and 2000ppm, headaches and drowsiness may occur, and ventilation is recommended;
  • above 2000ppm indicate significantly elevated CO2 levels and require immediate ventilation to improve indoor air quality;

So, I decided to build the IoT device on my favorite Raspberry Pi platform. For this project, I chose the fourth-generation Raspberry Pi 4 single-board computer.
Also I found a CO₂ sensor that produces an analog voltage signal. To process this data digitally, it first needs to be converted using an analog-to-digital converter (ADC).

1.Hardware part

Below are the links to the devices I used:

CO2 Sensor SKU SEN0159 

DFRobot I2C ADS1115 

All components are connected.

 

The GPIO shows which wire connects to which pin:

Actually, it looks as follows:

After many tries, I combined the example programs from both devices into a Python script to test the sensor.

A quick (and possibly boring) theory about the calibration curve:

The sensor outputs a voltage that decreases as CO₂ concentration increases. To convert voltage to ppm, we use a logarithmic calibration curve:

Where:

  • log_400 = 2.602 → log10(400 ppm), the baseline for clean air.
  • V_zero = ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE
  • slope = REACTION_VOLTAGE / (log_400 - log_1000)

This allows us to map measured voltages to actual CO₂ concentrations in ppm.

import time
import math
import sys
import os

sys.path.append(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__))))
from DFRobot_ADS1115 import ADS1115

# Device parameters
MG_PIN = 0  # define which analog input channel you are going to user
DC_GAIN = 8.5  # define the DC gain of amplifier

# Programm parameters
READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL = 0.05  # Interval between measurements (sec.)
READ_SAMPLE_TIMES = 5  # Sample numbers

# Calibration
ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE = 0.2936  # Default street voltage for 400ppm
REACTION_VOLTAGE = 0.030  # Voltage drop 1000ppm
CO2Curve = [2.602, ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE, (REACTION_VOLTAGE / (2.602 - 3))]

# Initialization ADS1115
ads1115 = ADS1115()
ads1115.set_addr_ADS1115(0x48)  # Set I2C adress
ads1115.set_gain(0x00)  # Set gain 6.144V

def read_adc(channel):
    """Reading ADS1115"""
    adc_val = ads1115.read_voltage(channel)
    return adc_val['r'] / 1000.0  # Transform to volts

def mg_read(mg_pin):
    """Reading average voltage"""
    v = 0
    for _ in range(READ_SAMPLE_TIMES):
        v += read_adc(mg_pin)
        time.sleep(READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL)
    v = v / READ_SAMPLE_TIMES
    return v

def mg_get_percentage(volts, pcurve):
    """Measurement CO₂ concentration в ppm"""
    if (volts / DC_GAIN) >= ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE:
        return -1
    else:
        return 10 ** (((volts / DC_GAIN) - pcurve[1]) / pcurve[2] + pcurve[0])

def ppm_to_percent(ppm):
    """Calculate ppm to percents"""
    return ppm / 10000.0  # 1 ppm = 0.0001%

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print("MG-811 Demo")
    try:
        while True:
            volts = mg_read(MG_PIN)
            print(f"SEN0159: {volts:.3f}V", end="  ")
            
            percentage = mg_get_percentage(volts, CO2Curve)
            if percentage == -1:
                print("CO2: <400 ppm (<0.04%)")
            else:
                percent = ppm_to_percent(percentage)
                print(f"CO2: {int(percentage)} ppm ({percent:.4f}%)")
            
            time.sleep(0.5)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("Program terminated by user")

Key Parameters from the script:

  • MG_PIN = 0 – Analog input channel for the MG-811 sensor.
  • DC_GAIN = 8.5 – Amplifier gain of the sensor.
  • READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL = 0.05 s – Time between measurements.
  • READ_SAMPLE_TIMES = 5 – Number of samples to average.
  • ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE = 0.2936 V – Voltage at 400 ppm CO₂ (clean air).
  • REACTION_VOLTAGE = 0.030 V – Voltage drop at 1000 ppm CO₂.

To determine ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE, I put the sensor outside in clean air.

Generate OData Web Service API after database table activation.

The following objects will be generated as part of an ABAP RAP managed scenario.

After generation and activation, I made a small change to enable auto-increment for the ID field and automatic timestamp insertion. For the Behavior Definition I enable “early numbering” and set “readonly” for the fields ID, CreatedAt, ChangedAt.

managed implementation in class ZBP_R_TSENSORDAT_GIV unique;
strict ( 2 );
extensible;
define behavior for ZR_TSENSORDAT_GIV alias ZrTsensordatGiv
persistent table ZTSENSORDAT_GIV
extensible
lock master
authorization master( global )
early numbering
{

  field ( readonly ) Id;
  field ( readonly ) CreatedAt;
  field ( readonly ) UpdatedAt;

  create;
  update;
  delete;

determination set_created_at on save { create; }
determination set_updated_at on modify { update; }

  mapping for ZTSENSORDAT_GIV corresponding extensible
  {
    ID = ID;
    Device = DEVICE;
    Voltage = VOLTAGE;
    Ppm = PPM;
    Percent = PERCENT;
    CreatedAt = CREATED_AT;
    UpdatedAt = UPDATED_AT;
  }
}

In the class referenced in the Behavior Definition (Local Types), implement logic for auto-increment and timestamp handling.

CLASS lhc_zr_tsensordat_giv DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

    METHODS earlynumbering_create FOR NUMBERING
      IMPORTING entities FOR CREATE ZrTsensordatGiv.

    METHODS get_global_authorizations FOR GLOBAL AUTHORIZATION
      IMPORTING REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR ZrTsensordatGiv RESULT result.

    METHODS set_created_at FOR DETERMINE ON SAVE
      IMPORTING keys FOR ZrTsensordatGiv~set_created_at.

    METHODS set_updated_at FOR DETERMINE ON MODIFY
      IMPORTING keys FOR ZrTsensordatGiv~set_updated_at.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS lhc_zr_tsensordat_giv IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD earlynumbering_create.

    DATA: entity TYPE STRUCTURE FOR CREATE Zr_Tsensordat_Giv,
          id_max TYPE ztsensordat_giv-id.

    " Copy entities w/o ID
    LOOP AT entities INTO entity WHERE id IS NOT INITIAL.
      APPEND CORRESPONDING #( entity ) TO mapped-zrtsensordatgiv.
    ENDLOOP.

    DATA(entities_wo_id) = entities.
    entities_wo_id[] = entities[].

    DELETE entities_wo_id WHERE Id IS NOT INITIAL.

    " Determine max ID
    SELECT MAX( Id ) FROM ztsensordat_giv INTO _max.

    LOOP AT entities_wo_id INTO entity.
      id_max += 1.
      entity-Id = id_max.

      entity-createdat = utclong_current( ).

      " Adding to the table
      APPEND CORRESPONDING #( entity ) TO mapped-zrtsensordatgiv.

      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = entity-%cid
                      %key = entity-%key ) TO mapped-zrtsensordatgiv.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_global_authorizations.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD set_created_at.

    READ ENTITIES OF zr_tsensordat_giv IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY zrtsensordatgiv
    FIELDS ( CreatedAt UpdatedAt )
    WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_sensordat).

    LOOP AT lt_sensordat ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_sensordat>).
      IF <fs_sensordat>-CreatedAt IS INITIAL.
        <fs_sensordat>-CreatedAt = utclong_current( ).
      ELSE.
        <fs_sensordat>-UpdatedAt = utclong_current( ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF zr_tsensordat_giv IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY zrtsensordatgiv
      UPDATE FIELDS ( CreatedAt UpdatedAt )
      WITH CORRESPONDING #( lt_sensordat ).

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD set_updated_at.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

Activate and publish the OData service.

Before testing the service, I create a class for test data generation:

CLASS zcl_insert_data_sensordat DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS generate_random_data
      IMPORTING iv_count TYPE i.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_insert_data_sensordat IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    DATA(lv_count) = 10.

    DELETE FROM ztsensordat_giv.

    generate_random_data( iv_count = lv_count ).

    COMMIT WORK.

    out->write( |[RAP] { lv_count } random entries have inserted into ZTSENSORDAT_GIV.| ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD generate_random_data.
    DATA: ls_sensordat_giv TYPE ztsensordat_giv,
          lv_time          TYPE utclong,
          lv_index         TYPE i,
          lv_voltage       TYPE f,
          lv_ppm           TYPE i,
          lv_percent       TYPE f.

    " Generators initialization
    DATA(lo_rand_int)   = cl_abap_random_int=>create( seed = cl_abap_random=>seed( ) ).
    DATA(lo_rand_float) = cl_abap_random_float=>create( seed = cl_abap_random=>seed( ) ).

    lv_time = utclong_current( ).

    DO iv_count TIMES.
      lv_index = sy-index.

      " Random generation
      lv_voltage = conv f( '1.5' ) + lo_rand_float->get_next( ) * conv f( '3.0' ). 
      lv_ppm     = 400 + lo_rand_int->get_next( ) MOD 601. " from 400 to 1000
      lv_percent = lv_ppm / 10000 * 100.              

      " Fill the table structure
      ls_sensordat_giv-id         = lv_index.
      ls_sensordat_giv-device     = |DEMO_SENSOR_{ lv_index }|.
      ls_sensordat_giv-voltage    = lv_voltage.
      ls_sensordat_giv-ppm        = lv_ppm.
      ls_sensordat_giv-percent    = lv_percent.
      ls_sensordat_giv-created_at = lv_time.
      ls_sensordat_giv-updated_at = lv_time.

      INSERT ztsensordat_giv FROM _sensordat_giv.
    ENDDO.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

Running the class will populate the table with random test data

Now we can test data retrieval.

Perfect, OData service works. At least for reading.

For the safe communication outside of SAP BTP platform, we need to create a XSUAA.

XSUAA (XS User Account and Authentication) is the authentication and authorization service of SAP Business Technology Platform.
It provides secure access to applications by issuing OAuth 2.0 JWT tokens, managing scopes and roles, and integrating with SAP Identity Authentication Service or external identity providers.
XSUAA is commonly used to protect APIs, applications, and service-to-service communication on SAP BTP.

We can find the service at Service Marketplace by name Authorization and Service management. We create an instance and service key.

The parameters will be defined in a JSON file.

{
  "xsappname": "xsuaa_iot",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "scopes": [
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.Viewer",
      "description": "Read access to sensor data"
    },
    {
      "name": "$XSAPPNAME.Admin",
      "description": "Admin access to sensor data"
    }
  ],
  "role-templates": [
    {
      "name": "Viewer",
      "description": "Read-Only Role",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.Viewer"]
    },
    {
      "name": "Admin",
      "description": "Full Access Role",
      "scope-references": ["$XSAPPNAME.Admin"]
    }
  ],
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "grant-types": [
      "password", 
      "authorization_code", 
      "client_credentials", 
      "refresh_token"
    ]
  },
  "authorities": []
}

Main parameters for the JSON are:

xsappname
Defines the technical name of the XSUAA application.
It is used as a namespace for scopes and roles.

“xsuaa_lot”

tenant-mode
Specifies whether the application is single-tenant or multi-tenant.
dedicated means one tenant per application instance.

“dedicated”

 scopes
Define what actions are allowed in the application.
Scopes are later included in OAuth JWT tokens.

“Viewer”, “Admin”

role-templates
Group scopes into business roles.
These roles are assigned to users via role collections.

oauth2-configuration
Defines which OAuth 2.0 grant types the application supports,
for example user login or service-to-service communication.

How tokens can be obtained.

  • authorization_code
    Used for interactive user login.
    Typical flow for UI applications like SAP Fiori or UI5, where the user is redirected to log in.
  • password
    Used when an application directly exchanges username and password for a token.
    Mainly for legacy or technical scenarios (generally discouraged today).
  • client_credentials
    Used for service-to-service communication.
    No user involved — the application authenticates itself.
  • refresh_token
    Allows the application to refresh an access token without forcing the user to log in again.

Authorities
Used to reference external authorities or technical privileges.

For more details, see the following link:

Application Security Descriptor Configuration Syntax 

I simplified this part and chose OAuth 2.0 Password authentication.

Once XSUAA instance is created, the respective XSUAA roles should be assigned to the technical user in the SAP BTP.

To connect to an SAP BTP OData service and send data, we first obtain a user token. With this token, we then request a CSRF token, which is used to securely send sensor data to the database.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) is a security mechanism that protects the backend from unauthorized data modifications. It ensures that write operations, such as POST or PUT requests, are performed only by trusted clients.

With the CSRF token, we can send requests to the OData service.

The following Python script reads data from the CO₂ sensor and sends it to the OData service.

import requests
import json
import time
import math
import sys
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

# XSUAA credentials
load_dotenv()

client_id = os.getenv("CLIENT_ID")
client_secret = os.getenv("CLIENT_SECRET")
token_url = os.getenv("TOKEN_URL")
odata_url = os.getenv("ODATA_URL")
odata_post_url = os.getenv("ODATA_GET_URL")

# User credentials
username = os.getenv("USERNAME")
password = os.getenv("PASSWORD")

# Device parameters
MG_PIN = 0  # define which analog input channel you are going to use
DC_GAIN = 8.5  # define the DC gain of amplifier

# Programm parameters
READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL = 0.05  # Interval between measurements (sec.)
READ_SAMPLE_TIMES = 5  # Sample numbers

# Calibration
ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE = 0.2936  # Default street voltage 400ppm
REACTION_VOLTAGE = 0.030  # Voltage drop 1000ppm
CO2Curve = [2.602, ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE, (REACTION_VOLTAGE / (2.602 - 3))]

# Initialization ADS1115
sys.path.append(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__))))
from DFRobot_ADS1115 import ADS1115
ads1115 = ADS1115()
ads1115.set_addr_ADS1115(0x48)  # Set I2C address
ads1115.set_gain(0x00)  # Set gain 6.144V

# Function to read the ADC value
def read_adc(channel):
    """Reading ADS1115"""
    adc_val = ads1115.read_voltage(channel)
    return adc_val['r'] / 1000.0  # Transform to volts

# Function to read the average voltage from the sensor
def mg_read(mg_pin):
    """Reading average voltage"""
    v = 0
    for _ in range(READ_SAMPLE_TIMES):
        v += read_adc(mg_pin)
        time.sleep(READ_SAMPLE_INTERVAL)
    v = v / READ_SAMPLE_TIMES
    return v

# Function to get CO₂ percentage based on voltage
def mg_get_percentage(volts, pcurve):
    """Measurement CO₂ concentration in ppm"""
    if (volts / DC_GAIN) >= ZERO_POINT_VOLTAGE:
        return -1
    else:
        return 10 ** (((volts / DC_GAIN) - pcurve[1]) / pcurve[2] + pcurve[0])

# Function to convert ppm to percentage
def ppm_to_percent(ppm):
    """Calculate ppm to percent"""
    return ppm / 10000.0  # 1 ppm = 0.0001%

# 1. Получение токена пользователя
session = requests.Session()

# 1. Getting User Token
token_response = session.post(
    token_url,
    data={
        "grant_type": "password",
        "username": username,
        "password": password,
        "client_id": client_id,
        "client_secret": client_secret
    },
    headers={"Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded"}
)

if token_response.status_code == 200:
    access_token = token_response.json().get("access_token")
    print("✅ User Token received")
else:
    print("❌ Token error:", token_response.status_code, token_response.text)
    exit()

# 2. Get CSRF Token

headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "X-CSRF-Token": "Fetch"
}

# Make a GET request to fetch the CSRF token
csrf_response = session.get(url=odata_url, headers=headers)

if csrf_response.status_code == 200:
    csrf_token = csrf_response.headers.get("X-CSRF-Token")
    print("✅ CSRF Token received:", csrf_token)
else:
    print("❌ Error getting CSRF Token:", csrf_response.status_code, csrf_response.text)
    exit()

# 2. OData API call URL
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Accept": "application/json",
    "X-CSRF-Token": csrf_token
}

# Set the interval for data sending (in seconds)
interval = 5  # по умолчанию 5 секунд

# Main loop to send data in a loop
try:
    while True:

        volts = mg_read(MG_PIN)
        voltage = volts
        #voltage = volts
        print(f"SEN0159: {volts:.3f}V", end="  ")

        percentage = mg_get_percentage(volts, CO2Curve)
        if percentage == -1:
            co2_value = 400
            percent = ppm_to_percent(co2_value)
            print("CO2: <400 ppm (<0.04%)")
        else:
            co2_value = int(percentage)
            percent = ppm_to_percent(co2_value)
            print(f"CO2: {co2_value} ppm ({percent:.4f}%)")
        
#        percent = f"{percent:.5f}"
        
#        print("Volts:", volts)
#        print("Voltage:", voltage)
#        print("CO2 value:", co2_value)
#        print("Percent:", percent)
        
        # Prepare the data for sending
        new_record = {
            "Device": "DFROBOT CO2 Sensor",
            "Voltage": round(volts,5),
            "Ppm": co2_value,
            "Percent": round(percent,5),
        }

#        # Prepare data for the new record (example)
#        new_record = {
#            "Device": "PYTHON_CO2_SENSOR",
#            "Voltage": 2.0100,
#            "Ppm": 1602,
#            "Percent": 0.1602,
#        }

        # Send POST request to add a new record
        response = session.post(url=odata_post_url, headers=headers, data=json.dumps(new_record))

        # Check the response
        print("Code:", response.status_code)
        print("Server response:", response.text)

        if response.status_code == 200 or response.status_code == 201:
            try:
#                print(response.json())
                print("✅ Successfully sent.")
            except requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError:
                print("❌ Error. No JSON response.")
        else:
            print("❌ Error response:", response.status_code, response.text)

        # Wait before sending the next request
        time.sleep(interval)

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Program terminated by user")

I placed the URLs and other parameters to .env file:

CLIENT_ID is taken from XSUAA service key

CLIENT_SECRET – from XSUAA service key

TOKEN_URL – from XSUAA service key

ODATA_URL – is taken from service URL, but for external non WEB access remove “-web” from the address

ODATA_GET_URL – is taken from the service URL + setting the entity set.

After execution, the Raspberry Pi begins sending data to the database table.

Our backend is ready.
